text: This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: 这里是BBC英语教学的《六分钟英语》节目。

text: I'm Neil.
zh: 我是尼尔。

text: And I'm Pippa.
zh: 我是皮帕。

text: Are you good at saving money, Neil, or do you like to spend it?
zh: 尼尔，你擅长存钱吗，还是喜欢花钱？

text: Well, actually a bit of both.
zh: 嗯，其实两者都有一点。

text: I like to spend money on nice things, but I also try to save mainly because I've got children.
zh: 我喜欢把钱花在好东西上，但我也会存钱，主要是因为我有孩子了。

text: How about you?
zh: 你呢？

text: Yeah, I'm the same.
zh: 是的，我也一样。

text: I like to save money, or I try to, for the future.
zh: 我喜欢存钱，或者说我努力存钱，为了将来。

text: But I also do spend it.
zh: 但我也确实会花钱。

text: I don't count every penny that I spend and save every single penny that I earn.
zh: 我不会把花的每一分钱都记下来，也不会把赚的每一分钱都存起来。

text: Hmm.
zh: 嗯。

text: Well, whether you're a saver or a spender, being in debt is common in the UK.
zh: 不管你是存钱的人还是花钱的人，负债在英国都很普遍。

text: Debt refers to money a person has borrowed to buy something and which they have to pay back, usually to a bank, a credit card company or another person.
zh: 债务指的是一个人为购买某样东西而借的钱，之后需要偿还，通常是还给银行、信用卡公司或其他人。

text: Many people avoid talking about debt, but it affects us all.
zh: 很多人都避免谈论债务，但它影响着我们所有人。

text: In 2025, over 1 ,000 people contacted the UK Citizens Advice Bureau every single day of the year for help with their debt.
zh: 2025年，每天都有超过1000人联系英国公民咨询局，寻求债务方面的帮助。

text: And when debt gets out of control, it causes stress and worry.
zh: 而当债务失控时，会带来压力和焦虑。

text: BBC Radio 4 programme Thinking Aloud interviewed one young man, Jason, about his debt.
zh: BBC 4台节目《Thinking Aloud》采访了一位年轻人杰森，谈他的债务问题。

text: Whatever jobs there are, aren't enough to provide for a family.
zh: 不管有什么工作，都不足以养家糊口。

text: Sometimes you need to take out loans.
zh: 有时候你不得不去借贷。

text: I've done it a few times.
zh: 我已经这样做过好几次了。

text: But you can't ever pay it back.
zh: 但你根本还不清。

text: You can't see a way out other than winning the lottery or something.
zh: 除了中彩票之类的事情，你看不到任何出路。

text: I'd like to think I could clear them all one day, even if it means like five years paying them off.
zh: 我愿意相信自己有一天能把债务全部还清，哪怕要花上五年时间来偿还。

text: Jason uses three phrases – payback, pay off and clear debt – all of which mean the same thing – to give back the money you've borrowed.
zh: 杰森用了三个短语——payback、pay off和clear debt——它们意思相同，都是指偿还你所借的钱。

text: In this episode, we'll hear more about living with debt by learning some useful new words and phrases.
zh: 在这一集里，我们将通过学习一些实用的新单词和短语，进一步了解负债生活。

text: And remember, you'll find all the vocabulary used, plus a quiz and worksheet, on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 记住，你可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到本期用到的所有词汇，还有配套的小测验和练习表。

text: But first, I have a question for you, Pippa.
zh: 不过首先，我有个问题要问你，皮帕。

text: According to debt support group The Money Charity, roughly how much is the average British adult in debt through credit cards, overdrafts and personal loans?
zh: 根据债务援助机构The Money Charity的数据，英国成年人平均通过信用卡、透支和个人贷款背负的债务大约是多少？
是a）2200英镑，b）4200英镑，还是c）6200英镑？

text: I'm not sure.
zh: 我不太确定。

text: I'll say £2 ,200.
zh: 我猜是2200英镑。

text: OK, well, we will find out the answer later in the programme.
zh: 好的，我们稍后会在节目中揭晓答案。

text: Jason's story features in a new book by sociologist Ryan Davey.
zh: 杰森的故事出现在社会学家瑞安·戴维的一本新书中。

text: Ryan spent months living in a low -income housing estate which he gave the fictional name Woldham.
zh: 瑞安曾在一个低收入住宅区生活了数月，他给这个地方取了个虚构的名字叫Woldham。

text: He talked with residents and listened to their money worries.
zh: 他与居民交谈，倾听他们对金钱的忧虑。

text: Here, Ryan explains more to BBC Radio 4's Thinking Aloud.
zh: 在这里，瑞安向BBC 4台的《Thinking Aloud》做了进一步说明。

text: My approach was to let people know that I was interested in learning about their lives and how they were making ends meet.
zh: 我的做法是让人们知道，我对了解他们的生活、以及他们如何维持生计很感兴趣。

text: So I did some interviews with residents and I paid attention to where debt came up in everyday conversations.
zh: 所以我采访了一些居民，并留意债务在日常对话中出现的情形。

text: For many people in Walden, Jason included, debt was an ordinary feature of daily life.
zh: 对沃尔登的许多人来说，包括杰森在内，债务是日常生活中很普通的一部分。

text: And beyond that, being in arrears, so being behind with one or more monthly payment commitment was part of daily life.
zh: 不仅如此，拖欠——也就是一项或多项每月还款义务逾期未还——也是日常生活的一部分。

text: The people of Walden were making ends meet.
zh: 沃尔登的居民们都在勉强维持生计。

text: To make ends meet means having just enough money to pay for basic living expenses like food, bills and rent.
zh: “勉强维持生计”指的是刚好有足够的钱支付基本生活开销，比如食物、账单和房租。

text: Ryan found that many residents were in arrears, a phrase meaning to still owe money that should have already been repaid.
zh: 瑞安发现许多居民都处于拖欠状态，这个短语的意思是仍欠着本该已经还清的钱。

text: For most residents, debt was a normal part of daily life.
zh: 对大多数居民来说，债务是日常生活中很正常的一部分。

text: With a regular income, debt can be managed, but for those who are unemployed or on low incomes, it can cause serious distress.
zh: 如果有稳定收入，债务是可以管理的，但对失业或低收入人群来说，这可能带来严重的困扰。

text: Here, Ryan discusses how the residents he met felt about their debt, with BBC Radio 4's Thinking Aloud.
zh: 在这里，瑞安向BBC 4台的《Thinking Aloud》谈到了他所接触的居民对债务的感受。

text: Jason actually fluctuated between wanting to clear all of his debts on the one hand and on the other,
zh: 杰森其实一直在两种情绪之间摇摆：一方面想把所有债务都还清，

text: what he described as living on the never -never and actually questioning the supposed moral obligation to pay his debts.
zh: 另一方面又过着他所说的“永远还不清”的日子，甚至质疑还债这种所谓的道德义务。

text: Over the months that I knew him, the financial strain on him and his partner increased.
zh: 在我认识他的这几个月里，他和伴侣所承受的经济压力不断增加。

text: They missed some of their bills, their internet was disconnected.
zh: 他们漏交了一些账单，家里的网络也被断了。

text: Jason was living on the never -never, an informal phrase for buying the things you need by making regular small payments over a long time.
zh: 杰森过着“the never-never”的生活，这是一个非正式短语，指通过长期定期支付小额款项来购买所需物品。

text: It's called the never -never because it seems the debt will never be repaid.
zh: 之所以叫“never-never”，是因为这笔债务似乎永远都还不完。

text: Jason experienced financial strain, emotional stress caused by a lack of money to meet his basic needs or to repay his debt.
zh: 杰森经历了财务压力，也就是因缺钱而无法满足基本需求或偿还债务所带来的情绪压力。

text: is a serious issue.
zh: 这是一个严重的问题。

text: It affects many people and there are support groups who can help if you need it.
zh: 它影响着许多人，如果你需要帮助，也有支持团体可以提供协助。

text: OK, Neil, what was the answer to your question?
zh: 好了，尼尔，你那个问题的答案是什么？

text: I asked what the average amount of debt a British adult has through credit cards, overdrafts and personal loans.
zh: 我问的是，英国成年人通过信用卡、透支和个人贷款所负的平均债务是多少。

text: I said it was around £2 ,200.
zh: 我猜是大约2200英镑。

text: I'm afraid that's not the right answer.
zh: 恐怕这不是正确答案。

text: In fact, it's £4 ,232.
zh: 实际上是4232英镑。

text: OK, let's recap the vocabulary we've learnt, starting with debt.
zh: 好的，我们来回顾一下今天学到的词汇，先从debt（债务）开始。

text: – money a person has borrowed and needs to give back.
zh: ——指一个人借来的、需要偿还的钱。

text: The phrases to pay back, to pay off and to clear a debt all mean to give back money you have borrowed.
zh: to pay back、to pay off和to clear a debt这几个短语的意思都是偿还你所借的钱。

text: If someone is making ends meet, they have just enough money to pay for basic living expenses.
zh: 如果有人make ends meet（勉强维持生计），意思是他们刚好有足够的钱支付基本生活开销。

text: And if they are in arrears, they still owe money that should have been repaid already.
zh: 如果他们in arrears（拖欠），意思是他们仍欠着本该已经还清的钱。

text: If you buy something on the never -never, you buy it by making small, regular payments over a long period.
zh: 如果你on the never-never（分期长期支付）购买东西，意思是通过长期、定期支付小额款项来购买。

text: And finally, financial strain is emotional stress caused by a lack of money to meet your basic needs.
zh: 最后，financial strain（财务压力）指的是因缺钱而无法满足基本需求所带来的情绪压力。

text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but if you'd like to know how debt is spelled, and all the other words from this episode, visit our website bbclearningenglish .com to find a full vocabulary list.
zh: 我们的六分钟又到时间了，如果你想知道debt这个词怎么拼写，以及本集出现的所有其他单词，请访问我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com，查看完整的词汇表。

text: See you again soon, but for now, it's goodbye.
zh: 我们很快再见，不过现在，先说再见。

text: Bye.
zh: 拜拜。
